---
---

:::{style="text-align: center;"}

**Research**

:::


My research explores the conditions under which religious nationalism, authoritarian populism, and divisive politics gain traction in democratic societies. Rather than focusing solely on elections, I examine the social movements, civil society organizations, and everyday political processes that enable populist leaders to build and sustain political support. Much of this work centers on India, where I study the evolution of Hindu nationalism as a grassroots movement and its implications for democracy, state-society relations, and majoritarian politics.

I also study patronage, welfare, and urban governance in marginalized communities across India. Drawing on extensive ethnographic fieldwork in urban informal settlements, I argue that everyday needs for public services—not simply ideological commitments—shape political participation and support for grassroots right-wing organizations. My research demonstrates how uneven state capacity influences political mobilization, particularly among women, helping explain variation in access to public services as well as the surprising success of Hindu nationalist organizations among the urban poor.

More recently, I have begun integrating computational social science into my research. I am exploring how text analysis, social network analysis, machine learning, and digital trace data can complement ethnographic and qualitative methods to study political rhetoric, social movements, polarization, and civic engagement in South Asia.
